name: Thyroid eye disease (Graves' orbitopathy)
presentation: Proptosis / exophthalmos — forward protrusion of the globe
Lid retraction — sclera visible above the iris (most common sign, 90%)
Gritty, red, watery eyes — exposure and inflammation
Diplopia — restrictive myopathy (inferior and medial recti most affected)
Colour vision change or visual acuity loss — implies compressive optic neuropathy (serious — urgent referral)
signs: Lid retraction (most common — 90%): white sclera visible above iris on primary gaze
Exophthalmos (most specific sign): Hertel exophthalmometry >21 mm or >2 mm asymmetry
Lid lag: upper lid lags on downgaze (von Graefe's sign)
Restricted extraocular movements — diplopia, worst on upgaze
Exposure keratopathy: corneal injection, punctate staining on fluorescein (most common complication)
Optic neuropathy: RAPD, reduced colour vision (red desaturation), reduced VA, field defect — most serious complication

bedside: Visual acuity (Snellen) + colour vision (Ishihara): reduced = compressive optic neuropathy → immediate referral
Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD): swinging-light test — present in optic nerve compression
Visual field assessment: central or pericentral scotoma in optic neuropathy
bloods: TFTs: TSH suppressed + elevated fT4/fT3 — TED can occur with euthyroid, hypo-, or hyperthyroid state
TSH-receptor antibodies (TRAb): elevated in Graves — high TRAb correlates with severity of TED
Thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPO Ab): positive in autoimmune thyroid disease
imaging: CT/MRI orbits: extraocular muscle enlargement (sparing tendons — differentiates from orbital pseudotumour), optic nerve compression at orbital apex — required before decompression surgery
CXR / chest CT: exclude sarcoidosis or lymphoma if presentation atypical

mx: Smoking cessation: single most important modifiable risk factor — smoking worsens severity and reduces response to treatment
Lubricants and eye protection: artificial tears, lubricating ointment at night, moisture chambers — for exposure symptoms
IV methylprednisolone: first-line for moderate-to-severe active TED — 12-week pulsed IV course; oral steroids less effective and more side effects
Orbital radiotherapy: adjunct to steroids in moderate-severe active disease with diplopia
Surgical rehabilitation (inactive disease only): orbital decompression (enlarges bony orbit) → squint surgery → eyelid surgery — always in this order
Urgent ophthalmology for sight-threatening TED: IV steroids; surgical decompression if no response in 24–48 h
traps: Colour vision loss (especially red desaturation) = optic neuropathy = urgent same-day ophthalmology referral — EUGOGO urgent review criteria includes unexplained VA loss, colour vision change, globe subluxation, corneal opacity, incomplete eyelid closure, disc swelling
Radioiodine (RAI) can worsen TED — if RAI is given to patients with active TED, cover with prednisolone to reduce risk of exacerbation
TED occurs in 25–50% of Graves' patients — but can occur with any thyroid status (hyper, hypo, or euthyroid); treating thyroid disease does not reliably improve eye disease
Exposure keratopathy = most common complication; optic neuropathy = most serious (irreversible blindness if untreated)
Lid retraction (most common sign) ≠ exophthalmos (most specific sign) — important distinction for MCQs
Surgical order is fixed: orbital decompression first → squint/diplopia surgery → eyelid surgery; only performed in inactive disease (CAS ≤2)
